content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

July 30, 2018

Natalie Dominguez
Managing Supervisor
Government Transparency
& Compliance Division
4181 Flat Rock Drive, Suite 305
Riverside, CA 92505

Re:     Your Request for Advice
        Our File No. A-18-066

Dear Ms. Dominguez:

     This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the campaign reporting provisions
of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

     Please note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) ]
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for
additional advice.

QUESTIONS

1. Are campaign consultants that are hired to prepare electronic campaign statements and
   reports on behalf of candidates and committees permitted to electronically sign on behalf
   of any member of a committee, including the candidate or treasurer?

2. Are wet signatures required to be submitted even when campaign statements and reports
   are filed electronically?

CONCLUSIONS

1. Campaign consultants are not permitted to electronically sign on behalf of any member
   of a committee, including the candidate or treasurer. The individual charged with
   verification of the campaign statement or report under the Act must sign the campaign
   statement or report.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-066
Page No. 2

2. Electronic signatures are an acceptable form of signature so long as the requirements of
Section 84615 are met.

FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR

You are the Division Manager at SouthTech Systems, a software development company
that is certified by the Secretary of State to electronically submit campaign statements and reports.
Your company has developed a specific procedure when signing and finalizing statements on the
assumption that all future electronic statements would be accepted as the original, and paper copies
would no longer be required.

Within your system, the electronic signature data comes from the user that is signed in such
as a candidate, treasurer or assistant treasurer. If someone is signed in as a candidate's treasurer,
that person cannot apply any other committee member's signature to the statement. SouthTech has
created "signature routing" within the filing system that would allow an individual that is signed in
to sign a statement and forward that statement to the other required signers. Upon the first person
adding their signature to the statement, the statement or report goes into a pending mode and
notifies the other signers via email that their signature is required to finalize the form.

Some clients are unhappy with the signature process you have put in place because they
cannot sign on behalf of others when signed in under their particular login. You would like to know
if it is permissible to allow a user to log in and apply any and all electronic signatures on behalf of
any member of the committee that is required to sign the campaign statement or report.

ANALYSIS

Under Section 81004 of the Act all reports and statements required to be filed under the Act
are required to be signed under penalty of perjury and verified by the filer. The verification must
state that the filer has used all reasonable diligence in its preparation, and that to the best of his
knowledge it is true and complete. Section 81004(b) further provides:

"A report or statement filed by a committee which qualifies under
subdivision (a) of Section 82013 shall be signed and verified by the treasurer [or
assistant treasurer], and a report or statement filed by any other person shall be
signed and verified by the filer. If the filer is an entity other than an individual, the
report or statement shall be signed and verified by a responsible officer of the
entity or by an attorney or a certified public accountant acting as agent for the
entity."

Additionally, under Section 84213:

"A candidate or state measure proponent shall verify his or her campaign
statement and the campaign statement of each committee subject to his or her
control. The verification shall be in accordance with provisions of Section 81004
except that it shall stat that to the best of his or her knowledge the treasurer of
each controlled committee used all reasonable diligence in the preparation of the
committee's statement."

File No. A-18-066
Page No. 3

Accordingly, the Act expressly requires campaign reports and statements to be signed and
verified by the specified treasurer, filer, candidate, or state measure proponent. While a responsible
officer, attorney, or certified public accountant acting as an agent may sign on behalf of an entity,
an agent or representative may not sign on behalf of an individual required to sign and verify the
campaign reports and statements. (Lojek Advice Letter, No. I-99-178). For example, a report or
statement for a committee that qualifies under subdivision (a) of Section 82013 must be signed and
verified by that committee's treasurer or assistant treasurer, and no one else may sign on behalf of
those individuals. Likewise, when a candidate is required to sign and verify a campaign statement,
no one else may sign on behalf of that candidate. This is true regardless of how an electronic filing
platform manages the sign in process for its users.

Section 84615 sets forth requirements for local jurisdiction electronic campaign filing
programs. Electronic filing under these programs is permissive, stating that a local
jurisdiction may require candidates or committees to file campaign statements or reports
electronically. Among other requirements, the program must accept filings in the standardized
record format developed by the Secretary of State, must ensure the integrity of the data transmitted,
must include a procedure for filers to comply with the requirement that they sign statements and
reports under penalty of perjury, and must generate a confirmation of filing.

Pursuant to Section 84615(j), a candidate or committee that files campaign statements or
reports electronically with a local jurisdiction is not required to file a paper copy of that document
with the local jurisdiction so long as all the requirements in Section 84615 are met. This assumes
the electronic filing system contains a procedure for filers to comply with the requirement that they
sign statements and reports under penalty of perjury under Section 81004, as required by Section
84615(g) for electronically filed statements.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Acting General Counsel

By: Sukhi K. Brar
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

SKB:jgl

sections.question:

reports on behalf of candidates and committees permitted to electronically sign on behalf 
of any member of a committee, including the candidate or treasurer? 
2. Are wet signatures required to be submitted even when campaign statements and reports 
are filed electronically?

Section 84615 are met.
